user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

Revocation of Authority 
201. Termination of agency .—An agency is terminated by the principal revoking his authority; or 
by the agent renouncing the business of the agency; or by the business of the agency being completed; or 
by either the principal or agent dying or becoming of unsound mind; or by the principal being adjudicated 
an insolvent under the provisions of any Act for the time being in force for the relief of insolvent debtors. 
202.Termination of agency, where agent has an interest in subject-matter.—Where the agent has 
himself an interest in the property which forms the subject-matter of the agency, the agency cannot, in the 
absence of an express contract, be terminated to the prejudice of such interest. 
Illustrations 
(a) A gives authority to B to sell A ’s land, and to pay himself, out of the proceeds, the debts due to him from A. A cannot 
revoke this authority, nor can it be terminated by his insanity or death.

Revocation of Authority 
201. Termination of agency .—An agency is terminated by the principal revoking his authority; or 
by the agent renouncing the business of the agency; or by the business of the agency being completed; or 
by either the principal or agent dying or becoming of unsound mind; or by the principal being adjudicated 
an insolvent under the provisions of any Act for the time being in force for the relief of insolvent debtors. 
202.Termination of agency, where agent has an interest in subject-matter.—Where the agent has 
himself an interest in the property which forms the subject-matter of the agency, the agency cannot, in the 
absence of an express contract, be terminated to the prejudice of such interest. 
Illustrations 
(a) A gives authority to B to sell A ’s land, and to pay himself, out of the proceeds, the debts due to him from A. A cannot 
revoke this authority, nor can it be terminated by his insanity or death.

limitation.from: When the contract is broken (date of illegal termination)
limitation.description: For compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied

commercial.heading: IN THE COURT OF THE {court_type} (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
procedural: Section 12A pre-institution mediation (mandatory unless urgent interim relief sought)
Statement of truth as per Commercial Courts Act

Particularised damages schedule showing: (a) methodology for computing loss of profits, (b) basis for goodwill valuation, (c) itemised capital investment with documentary proof, (d) unsold stock valuation at cost or market value whichever is lower

lkb_brief.coa_guidance: The cause of action is a SINGLE EVENT — illegal termination. Do NOT plead 'continuing breach' because termination is a one-time act. Three-part CoA: (1) first arose on date of termination letter, (2) further arose when defendant failed to respond to protest/legal notice, (3) cause is NOT continuing — but defendant's retention of benefits of plaintiff's investment and market development continues, and plaintiff's right to damages subsists.
lkb_brief.interest_guidance: Defendant retained the benefit of plaintiff's capital investment and market development. Interest justified at commercial rate reflecting the reasonable return plaintiff would have earned on invested capital.

mandatory_inline_sections.instruction: Include a separate paragraph stating compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015: 'The Plaintiff states that in compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, pre-institution mediation was attempted/undertaken through {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} but the same was unsuccessful / the Defendant failed to participate.' If urgent interim relief is sought, state: 'The Plaintiff seeks urgent interim relief and is therefore exempt from the requirement of pre-institution mediation under Section 12A.' This is MANDATORY for Commercial Court suits.

Include a STATEMENT OF TRUTH as required by the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. Format: 'STATEMENT OF TRUTH\n\nI, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby state that the facts stated in paragraphs 1 to [last para] of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not wilfully suppressed any material fact. I understand that I may be liable for proceedings under the Indian Penal Code / Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita for making a false statement.' This is MANDATORY for Commercial Court suits.

Include a DETAILED DAMAGES SCHEDULE as required by the Commercial Courts Act. For each damages category, show: (a) head of damage, (b) amount claimed, (c) methodology/basis for computation, (d) documentary proof reference. Cover: loss of profit (projected from historical data), goodwill (based on territory value, brand equity, customer base), wasted capital (itemised with invoices/receipts), unsold stock (at cost or market value, whichever lower).

draft_artifacts.title: Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR ILLEGAL TERMINATION OF DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT

UNDER SECTION 26 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, 1908

READ WITH SECTION 73 AND SECTION 74 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued. The Plaintiff is a registered dealer/distributor engaged in the business of {{BUSINESS_TYPE}} and the Defendant is a manufacturer/supplier of {{PRODUCT_TYPE}}.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. Further, the Dealership Agreement was executed at {{EXECUTION_PLACE}} within the jurisdiction of this Court, and the illegal termination notice was received by the Plaintiff at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} within the jurisdiction of this Court.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes of this nature under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that in compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, pre-institution mediation was attempted/undertaken through {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} but the same was unsuccessful as the Defendant failed to participate / refused to settle the dispute. The Plaintiff has obtained the necessary certificate of non-settlement / exemption as required under the said Act, thereby fulfilling the mandatory procedural requirement before instituting this suit.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. The Plaintiff is a reputable business entity residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} and has been engaged in the distribution and sale of {{PRODUCT_TYPE}} for the past several years. The Plaintiff has established a significant market presence and customer base in the territory of {{TERRITORY_NAME}} through substantial effort and investment.

6. The Defendant is engaged in the manufacturing and supply of {{PRODUCT_TYPE}} and is known to the Plaintiff through business dealings. On or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a written Dealership Agreement whereby the Plaintiff was appointed as an authorized dealer for the Defendant's products in the territory of {{TERRITORY_NAME}} (a true copy whereof is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A).

7. Under the terms of the Dealership Agreement, the Plaintiff was obligated to promote the Defendant's products, maintain stock levels, and achieve sales targets. In consideration thereof, the Defendant was obligated to supply goods at agreed rates, provide marketing support, and not terminate the agreement without valid cause and prior notice as per the terms stipulated in the contract.

8. In pursuance of the said Dealership Agreement and in good faith reliance upon the Defendant's representations, the Plaintiff invested substantial capital amounting to Rs. {{INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/- towards infrastructure development, inventory procurement, staffing, and market development in the agreed territory (proof of the said investment, being invoices and financial records, is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

9. The Plaintiff duly performed all obligations under the Agreement, achieved the stipulated sales targets, and maintained the required stock levels. The Plaintiff developed significant goodwill for the Defendant's brand in the territory, creating a customer base that generated consistent revenue for the Defendant over the period of {{DURATION YEARS}} yea {{AMOUNT}}. However, despite the Plaintiff having duly and fully performed his part of the obligation, the Defendant arbitrarily and illegally terminated the Dealership Agreement vide letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} without any valid cause, without providing prior notice as contractually required, and without offering any opportunity to cure alleged breaches (a true copy of the termination letter is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C).

10. Subsequent to the Defendant's illegal termination, the Plaintiff made repeated oral requests and written communications to the Defendant on {{FOLLOWUP_DATES}}, urging the Defendant to revoke the termination and honour the contractual obligations. Despite these repeated reminders, the Defendant continued to evade and delay without any lawful justification (copies of the correspondence are annexed hereto and marked as Annexure D).

11. Left with no alternative, the Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be served upon the Defendant through registered post A.D., calling upon the Defendant to compensate the Plaintiff for the illegal termination and restore the dealership within 15 days of receipt of the said notice (a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure E). Despite receipt of the said legal notice, the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the demands therein.

LEGAL BASIS

12. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 73 provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation from the party who has broken the contract for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach. In the present case, the Defendant's illegal termination caused the Plaintiff loss of profit, wasted capital investment, and loss of goodwill, which are direct consequences of the breach.

13. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 74 provides that when a contract has been broken, if a sum is named in the contract as the amount to be paid in case of such breach, or if the contract contains any other stipulation by way of penalty, the party complaining of the breach is entitled to receive from the party who has broken the contract reasonable compensation not exceeding the amount so named or, as the case may be, the penalty stipulated for. The termination clause in the Agreement stipulated conditions for termination which were not followed, thereby triggering liability for reasonable compensation for the breach of such stipulation.

14. Without prejudice to the above and in the alternative, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is also liable under Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 39 provides that when a party to a contract has refused to perform, or disabled himself from performing, his promise in its entirety, the promisee may put an end to the contract, unless he has signified, by words or conduct, his willingness to continue its operation. The Defendant's refusal to supply goods and illegal termination amounted to refusing to perform the promise in its entirety, entitling the Plaintiff to claim damages for the resulting loss.

15. It is further submitted that no oral variation of the Agreement was ever entered into between the parties. The termination clause was not complied with as no prior notice was given, no opportunity to cure was provided, and no valid ground for termination existed. The Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development without compensation, thereby enriching himself at the expense of the Plaintiff.

CAUSE OF ACTION

16. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant illegally terminated the Dealership Agreement vide letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} without valid cause or notice, thereby breaching the said agreement and giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for damages.

17. The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} served through registered post A.D., failed and refused to comply with the demands therein. The cause of action is a single event based on the illegal termination; however, the loss subsists as the Defendant continues to retain the benefit of the Plaintiff's investment and market development without compensation.

LIMITATION

18. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years for compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied. The cause of action having first arisen on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant breached the agreement, and the suit being filed within the prescribed period from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

19. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act, computed on the suit valuation at the applicable ad valorem rate.

INTEREST

20. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows, the Defendant having wrongfully retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development:

    (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit, amounting to approximately Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}, the said rate being justified on the ground that the Defendant has wrongfully retained the Plaintiff's capital which the Plaintiff could have otherwise deployed in business at prevailing commercial rates;

    (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;

    (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization.

PRAYER

21. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for damages amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the compensation for illegal termination of the Dealership Agreement;

    (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the said sum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit;

    (c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit;

    (d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;

    (e) Award costs of this suit including court fees, advocate fees and all pre-litigation costs incurred for issuing the legal notice;

    (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS, THE PLAINTIFF SHALL, AS IN DUTY BOUND, EVER PRAY.

PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

22. In compliance with the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, the Plaintiff provides the following particularised damages schedule showing the methodology for computation:

    (a) Loss of Profit for Unexpired Term: Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Computed based on average monthly profit of Rs. {{AVG_MONTHLY_PROFIT}}/- derived from historical financial data (Annexure F) multiplied by the remaining months of the agreement term ({{REMAINING_MONTHS}} months).
        Documentary Proof: Annexure F (Financial Statements), Annexure A (Agreement Term).

    (b) Loss of Goodwill: Rs. {{GOODWILL_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Based on territory value, brand equity developed, and customer base established over {{DURATION YEARS}} years, valued at {{VALUATION_MULTIPLE}} times the average annual turnover.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure G (Market Valuation Report), Annexure H (Customer List).

    (c) Wasted Capital Investment: Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Itemised capital investment in infrastructure, inventory, and staffing that is now redundant due to termination, valued at cost less depreciation.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure B (Invoices and Receipts), Annexure I (Asset Register).

    (d) Loss on Unsold Stock: Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Valuation of unsold stock at cost or market value, whichever is lower, as the stock is specific to the Defendant's brand and cannot be sold elsewhere.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure J (Stock Statement), Annexure K (Valuation Certificate).

    (e) Future Business Opportunities: Rs. {{FUTURE_OPPORTUNITY_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Reasonable estimation of lost opportunities due to reputational damage and inability to secure alternative dealership immediately.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure L (Correspondence with other principals).

    Total Claim: Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

| Sr. No. | Description | Annexure |

|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1. | Dealership Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} | Annexure A |
| 2. | Proof of Capital Investment (Invoices/Receipts) | Annexure B |
| 3. | Termination Letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} | Annexure C |
| 4. | Correspondence/Reminders sent to Defendant | Annexure D |
| 5. | Legal Notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with Postal Receipt | Annexure E |
| 6. | Financial Statements for Profit Computation | Annexure F |
| 7. | Market Valuation Report for Goodwill | Annexure G |
| 8. | Customer List for Goodwill Basis | Annexure H |
| 9. | Asset Register for Capital Investment | Annexure I |
| 10. | Stock Statement for Unsold Stock | Annexure J |
| 11. | Valuation Certificate for Stock | Annexure K |
| 12. | Correspondence for Future Opportunities | Annexure L |

VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby solemnly verify and state on oath that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 23 of this plaint are true and correct to my personal knowledge based upon the records and documents available with me, that the contents relating to legal submissions are based upon legal advice received and are believed to be correct, that no part of the cause of action has arisen within the jurisdiction of any other court, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                                        PLAINTIFF

STATEMENT OF TRUTH

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby state that the facts stated in paragraphs 1 to 23 of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not wilfully suppressed any material fact. I understand that I may be liable for proceedings under the Indian Penal Code / Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita for making a false statement.

                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                                        PLAINTIFF

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}

Left with no alternative, the Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be served upon the Defendant through registered post A.D., calling upon the Defendant to compensate the Plaintiff for the illegal termination and restore the dealership within 15 days of receipt of the said notice (a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure E). Despite receipt of the said legal notice, the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the demands therein.

LEGAL BASIS

12. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 73 provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation from the party who has broken the contract for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach. In the present case, the Defendant's illegal termination caused the Plaintiff loss of profit, wasted capital investment, and loss of goodwill, which are direct consequences of the breach.

13. Without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is also liable under Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 39 provides that when a party to a contract has refused to perform, or disabled himself from performing, his promise in its entirety, the promisee may put an end to the contract, unless he has signified, by words or conduct, his willingness to continue its operation. The Defendant's refusal to supply goods and illegal termination amounted to refusing to perform the promise in its entirety, entitling the Plaintiff to claim damages for the resulting loss.

14. It is further submitted that no oral variation of the Agreement was ever entered into between the parties. The termination clause was not complied with as no prior notice was given, no opportunity to cure was provided, and no valid ground for termination existed. The Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development without compensation, thereby enriching himself at the expense of the Plaintiff.

CAUSE OF ACTION

15. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant illegally terminated the Dealership Agreement vide letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} without valid cause or notice, thereby breaching the said agreement and giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for damages.

16. The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} served through registered post A.D., failed and refused to comply with the demands therein. The cause of action is a single event based on the illegal termination; however, the loss subsists as the Defendant continues to retain the benefit of the Plaintiff's investment and market development without compensation.

17. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years for compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied. The cause of action having first arisen on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant breached the agreement, and the suit being filed within the prescribed period from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

18. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. 19. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows, the Defendant having wrongfully retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development:

    (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit, amounting to approximately Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}, the said rate being justified on the ground that the Defendant has wrongfully retained the Plaintiff's capital which the Plaintiff could have otherwise deployed in business at prevailing commercial rates;

    (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;

    (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization.

20. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for damages amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the compensation for illegal termination of the Dealership Agreement;

    (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the said sum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit;

    (c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit;

    (d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;

    (e) Award costs of this suit including court fees, advocate fees and all pre-litigation costs incurred for issuing the legal notice;

    (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS, THE PLAINTIFF SHALL, AS IN DUTY BOUND, EVER PRAY.

21. In compliance with the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, the Plaintiff provides the following particularised damages schedule showing the methodology for computation:

    (a) Loss of Profit for Unexpired Term: Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Computed based on average monthly profit of Rs. {{AVG_MONTHLY_PROFIT}}/- derived from historical financial data (Annexure F) multiplied by the remaining months of the agreement term ({{REMAINING_MONTHS}} months).
        Documentary Proof: Annexure F (Financial Statements), Annexure A (Agreement Term).

    (b) Loss of Goodwill: Rs. {{GOODWILL_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Based on territory value, brand equity developed, and customer base established over {{DURATION_YEARS}} years, valued at {{VALUATION_MULTIPLE}} times the average annual turnover.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure G (Market Valuation Report), Annexure H (Customer List).

    (c) Wasted Capital Investment: Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Itemised capital investment in infrastructure, inventory, and staffing that is now redundant due to termination, valued at cost less depreciation.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure B (Invoices and Receipts), Annexure I (Asset Register).

    (d) Loss on Unsold Stock: Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_AMOUNT}}/-
        Methodology: Valuation of unsold stock at cost or market value, whichever is lower, as the stock is specific to the Defendant's brand and cannot be sold elsewhere.
        Documentary Proof: Annexure J (Stock Statement), Annexure K (Valuation Certificate).

9. The Plaintiff duly performed all obligations under the Agreement, achieved the stipulated sales targets, and maintained the required stock levels.
